Backtesting using vectorbt — cookbook (Part 5)

Tobi Lux
22 min read
·
Feb 6, 2026

This is Part-5 of a cookbook for using essential elements of vectorbt (vbt) for backtesting. Note: my statements are valid for the free version of vbt only (version 0.28.1).

As always, the full code is available on my GitHub — see also the disclaimer at the end.

Content of the blog

This series of blogs has the following content:

  • Part 1: Setting the scene, getting started with vbt, Overview on methods and parameters (for calculation of metrics, plotting, logging etc.)
  • Part 2: trade-prices (default/modified), impact of parameters as size_type, size, min_size, max_size, fees, slippage, standard StopLoss and TakeProfit-behaviour
  • Part 3: callback-functions in vbt, using callbacks for customizing Stops
  • Part 4 : multiple orders per position, impact of accumulation, accept_partial, StopLoss and TakeProfit for multiple orders, ShortOnly trades, LongShort trades
  • Part 5 : (this part) multi-ticker portfolio and standard outputs, cash distributions, cash-pooling (with and without order size limitations), groups of tickers, group-cash-pools (with and without order size-limitations), LongShortStrategies in multiticker-portfolios
  • Part 6: some use-cases as batch-testing of different strategies, optimisation of parameters of trading strategies, sector rotation, etc…

1. Data Download

For a multi-ticker portfolio, we first need to retrieve historical price data for all selected assets. As in the previous sections, we rely on the standard yfinance functionality to download the data. From the retrieved dataset, we extract the individual OHLC (Open, High, Low, Close) components.

Each price component is stored in its own DataFrame, using the date as the index and the corresponding ticker symbols as column names. This structure allows for clean, consistent handling of multiple assets and simplifies subsequent analysis.

# download OHLC-data
tickers = ['PFE', 'GOOG', 'MSFT', 'MRNA']
start_date = '2022-01-01'
end_date = '2025-10-11'
prices = yf.download(tickers, start=start_date, end=end_date, auto_adjust=True)

open = prices.Open
high = prices.High
low = prices.Low
close = prices.Close

We selected four tickers from two distinct market segments — Technology and Pharmaceuticals — which exhibit markedly different behaviors. One clear illustration of these differences is their value development over time.

2. The SMA/RSI-strategy and their signals

We will show the power of a Portfolio-instance, generated by the class-method from_signals(...) by implementing a strategy, that will deliver a large number of signals.

Entry- and Exits Signals are generated based on different constellations of fast-SMA, slow-SMA and the RSI. The code snippet below is self explaining. We will call this SMA/RSI-Strategy.

Note: Our tasks is to demonstrate the behaviour of a vbt Portfolioinstance, rather than presenting a brilliant strategy. Again, nobody would seriously consider implementing this strategy for trading in real life, however, it serves well for demonstration.

# strategy parameter
window_fast = 10
window_slow = 50
window_rsi = 14

# calculation of indicators, required for the strategy
fast_sma = pd.DataFrame(index=close.index, columns=close.columns)
slow_sma = pd.DataFrame(index=close.index, columns=close.columns)
rsi = pd.DataFrame(index=close.index, columns=close.columns)

for ticker in tickers:
    fast_sma[ticker] = ta.trend.sma_indicator(close[ticker], window=window_fast, fillna=False)
    slow_sma[ticker] = ta.trend.sma_indicator(close[ticker], window=window_slow, fillna=False)
    rsi[ticker] = ta.momentum.rsi(close[ticker], window=14, fillna=False)

# generation of signals
long_entries  = (fast_sma > slow_sma) & (rsi > 30)
long_exits    = (fast_sma < slow_sma) | (rsi < 70)
short_entries = (fast_sma < slow_sma) & (rsi < 70)
short_exits   = (fast_sma > slow_sma) | (rsi > 30)

As a result, we obtain a separate DataFrame for each type of signal, with dates as the index and ticker symbols as the columns.

Let’s briefly recap our approach so far:

  • In the previous sections, we started with a strategy based on SMA crossovers. This approach relied on sequential, alternating signals, meaning that an exit signal strictly followed an entry signal, and vice versa.
  • We then implemented a mean-reversion strategy using Bollinger Band touches. This strategy was based on non-alternating signals, where multiple entry signals could occur before an exit (and vice versa).
  • Both of these strategies were event-driven: signals were generated only at the precise moment an event occurred (such as a crossing or a band touch).

In contrast, the signals we generate now are purely condition-based rather than event-based. As long as a given condition remains true, the same signal may be emitted over multiple consecutive bars. Moreover, it is possible to generate multiple signals within a single bar.

Handling and resolving these potentially overlapping or conflicting signals is delegated entirely to the Portfolio instance.

To illustrate this behavior, we begin by examining the total number of generated signals. It is evident, that we get a significant higher number of signals, compared to the previous strategies:

As already mentioned, we observe many bars that generate more than one signal.

In some cases, multiple signals on the same bar are conflicting:

  • Long entry and long exit
  • Short entry and short exit
  • Long entry and short entry
  • Long exit and short exit

In other cases, multiple signals on the same bar may actually reinforce a particular market direction:

  • Long exit and short entry — supports taking a short position
  • Short exit and long entry — supports taking a long position

This is not merely an academic consideration. When we examine the number of bars with multiple signals generated by our strategy, we find that such situations occur frequently. In the following sections, we will see how vbt handles these types of signal conflicts internally.

3. Baseline Portfolio

Let’s start by defining a baseline portfolio that will serve as a reference point:

  • Multi-ticker portfolio
  • Long-only positions
  • Equal initial cash allocation for each ticker
  • No cash sharing (cash is managed separately for each ticker)

A multi-ticker Portfolio instance with these characteristics can be created as follows:

# equal cash-distribution
total_cash = 40_000
init_cash_per_ticker = total_cash / len(tickers)

# generation of a baseline portfolio instance (multiple tickers)
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,                    # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries=long_entries,           # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits=long_exits,               # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    #short_entries=short_entries,   # no short-entry
    #short_exits=short_exits,       # no short-exit
    init_cash=init_cash_per_ticker, # evenly distributed inital cash
    freq='D'                        # daily evaluation of metrics
)

What about the conflicts mentioned above?

In a long-only portfolio, there is only one possible type of conflict: receiving a long entry and a long exit signal on the same bar. The behavior in this situation is controlled by the upon_long_conflict parameter. By default, it is set to "Ignore", meaning that both conflicting signals are disregarded.

Other available options include:

  • "Entry": On a long conflict, execute the entry and ignore the exit
  • "Exit": On a long conflict, ignore the entry and execute the exit (a very risk-averse choice in times of uncertainty)
  • "Adjacent": On a long conflict, execute both signals as separate orders
  • "Opposite": On a long conflict, execute the action opposite to the current position

These options allow for fairly sophisticated handling of conflicting signals. We will revisit this topic in more detail when discussing long–short, multi-ticker portfolios.

Now, let’s review the standard outputs of a multi-ticker portfolio.

Outputs: List of Trading information and Trading Activity Plots

We can use the same reports and plots as in the previous parts. Se the example of the key-information of the trades. All trade informations are compiled in portfolio.trades.records_readable .

pf.trades.records_readable.columns

# Output is: Index(['Exit Trade Id', 'Column', 'Size', 'Entry Timestamp', 'Avg Entry Price',
#       'Entry Fees', 'Exit Timestamp', 'Avg Exit Price', 'Exit Fees', 'PnL',
#       'Return', 'Direction', 'Status', 'Position Id'],
#      dtype='object')
#

To get the trades of one ticker in the multi-ticker portfolio, we can filter by the column ‘Column’ that contains the ticker-names.

To visualize the trades, we can use the built-in plotting functionality provided by vbt . Since these plots operate on a single ticker at a time, we need to apply them separately for each asset by addressing pf[ticker].

# plotting trades of the individual tickers
n_cols = len(pf.wrapper.columns)

fig = make_subplots(rows=n_cols, cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True, subplot_titles=[f"Trades for {col}" for col in pf.wrapper.columns],
    vertical_spacing=0.05
)

for i, col in enumerate(pf.wrapper.columns, start=1):
    trade_fig = pf[col].plot_trades()
    for trace in trade_fig.data:
        trace.showlegend = (i == 1)
        fig.add_trace(trace, row=i, col=1)

fig.update_layout( height=200 * n_cols, width=1000,
    title="Trades Overview", showlegend=True
)
fig.show()

Outputs: Statistical Parameters

Calling pf.stats() returns aggregated statistics across all tickers in the portfolio. This aggregation is performed by simple averaging. While averaging is meaningful for some metrics (such as profits), it can produce less informative or even misleading results for others (for example, drawdowns).

In addition to the aggregated view, it is often useful to inspect the statistics of individual tickers. These are available via pf[ticker].stats().

In the code below, we first compute the aggregated statistics and rename the corresponding column. We then concatenate, ticker by ticker, the statistical metrics for each individual asset to obtain a comprehensive overview.

# stats per ticker and overall stats
stats = pf.stats()
stats.name='Portfolio (agg)'

for ticker in tickers:
    stats = pd.concat([stats, pf[ticker].stats()], axis=1)
display(stats)

We get the following statistical parameters (as rows of the DataFrame):

Index(['Start', 'End', 'Period', 'Start Value', 'End Value',
       'Total Return [%]', 'Benchmark Return [%]', 'Max Gross Exposure [%]',
       'Total Fees Paid', 'Max Drawdown [%]', 'Max Drawdown Duration',
       'Total Trades', 'Total Closed Trades', 'Total Open Trades',
       'Open Trade PnL', 'Win Rate [%]', 'Best Trade [%]', 'Worst Trade [%]',
       'Avg Winning Trade [%]', 'Avg Losing Trade [%]',
       'Avg Winning Trade Duration', 'Avg Losing Trade Duration',
       'Profit Factor', 'Expectancy', 'Sharpe Ratio', 'Calmar Ratio',
       'Omega Ratio', 'Sortino Ratio'],
      dtype='object')

The parameters are calculated as aggregated values and for each ticker individually and forms the columns of the DataFrame:

Index(['Portfolio (agg)', 'PFE', 'GOOG', 'MSFT', 'MRNA'], dtype='object')

Outputs: timelines of portfolio metrics

A. Development of Portfolio Value Over Time

Next, we analyze the time-dependent behavior of portfolio metrics and parameters. As an example, consider the portfolio value.

By calling pf.value(), we obtain a DataFrame showing the evolution of each ticker’s value over time. These timelines can then be visualized using the built-in plotting methods provided by vbt, offering an intuitive view of how the portfolio and its components perform throughout the period.

pf.value().vbt.plot(
    width=800, height=300,
    title= 'Value of the portfolio members',
    ).show()

All the parameters introduced earlier are also accessible. For example, we can retrieve the final cumulative returns for each ticker as follows:

pf.cumulative_returns().iloc[-1].rename('final returns (EUR)')

Since pf.value() returns a DataFrame containing the values of all tickers, the total portfolio value must be calculated separately by summing across columns using .sum(axis=1). We can then visualize the resulting total portfolio value using the built-in vectorbt plotting functions.

The final portfolio value corresponds to the last entry of this time series. From this value, we can also compute the overall profit, both in absolute terms (EUR) and as a percentage.

# calculating total portfolio value
total_value = pf.value().sum(axis=1)
total_value.vbt.plot(width=800, height=250, title= 'Total value of portfolio').show()

print(f'final total_value:  {total_value.iloc[-1]:,.2f} EUR')
print(f'final profit :      {total_value.iloc[-1]-total_cash:,.2f} EUR')
print(f'final profit %:     {(total_value.iloc[-1]-total_cash)/total_cash*100:,.2f} %')
final total_value:  38,013.32 EUR
final profit :      -1,986.68 EUR
final profit %:     -4.97 %

B. Development of Number of Assets Over Time

We won’t cover all possible timelines here (for more examples, see the GitHub repository). As an example, we will look at the number of assets held per ticker over time.

Once again, we can visualize this using the built-in plotting functions of vbt:

# plotting development of assets of the individual tickers
n_cols = len(pf.wrapper.columns)

fig = make_subplots(rows=n_cols, cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True, subplot_titles=[f"Assets for {col}" for col in pf.wrapper.columns],
    vertical_spacing=0.06
)

for i, col in enumerate(pf.wrapper.columns, start=1):
    trace_fig = pf[col].plot_assets()
    for trace in trace_fig.data:
        fig.add_trace(trace, row=i, col=1)

fig.update_layout( height=120 * n_cols, width=1000,
    title="Assets Overview", showlegend=False
)

C. Development of Drawdowns Over Time

By calling pf.drawdown(), we obtain a DataFrame that shows the individual drawdowns for each ticker.

Alternatively, we can use vbt built-in underwater plots to create a dashboard, visualizing the drawdowns of all portfolio members in a single, intuitive view.

# underwater plots of the individual tickers
n_cols = len(pf.wrapper.columns)

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=n_cols, cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True,
    subplot_titles=[f"Underwater for {col}" for col in pf.wrapper.columns],
    vertical_spacing=0.05
)

for i, col in enumerate(pf.wrapper.columns, start=1):
    trace_fig = pf[col].plot_underwater()
    for trace in trace_fig.data:
        trace.showlegend = (i == 1)
        fig.add_trace(trace, row=i, col=1)

fig.update_layout(
    height=120 * n_cols, width=1000,
    title="Dashboard Underwater-Plots", showlegend=True
)

However, in a portfolio, we are primarily interested in the total drawdown. This cannot be calculated directly from the individual drawdowns of each ticker. Instead, we need to consider the total portfolio value — the sum of the values of all tickers.

From the time series of the total portfolio value, we can then compute the drawdown using the standard formulas, as follows:

# calculate total value and from that the drawdown
total_value = pf.value().sum(axis=1)
rolling_max = total_value.cummax()
drawdown = (total_value - rolling_max) / rolling_max

# plotting
fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=drawdown.index, y=drawdown,
        mode='lines', name='Drawdown', fill='tozeroy', fillcolor='LightCoral',
        ),
    )
# finetuning
fig.update_layout(
    title='Portfolio Drawdown',
    xaxis_title='Date', yaxis_title='Drawdown', hovermode='x unified',
    width=1200, height=250
)
fig.show()

D. Timely Evolution of Portfolio Positions

When evaluating a strategy, it is often useful to visualize the positions of all portfolio members over time in a single dashboard. To facilitate this, we have created a function plot_portfolio_positions(...) (details are available in the GitHub repository).

This function is based on the sign of the net exposure, as explained in the previous sections, and provides an intuitive view of how positions evolve across all tickers throughout the trading period.

Portfolio with Individual Allocation of Initial Cash

We now create a portfolio instance with the following features:

  • Multi-ticker portfolio
  • Long-only positions
  • Distribution of total cash across tickers according to specified weights
  • No cash sharing

Important note: The sequence of tickers in tickers (e.g., when using yf.download) does not necessarily match the sequence of tickers in the Portfolio object, where tickers are automatically sorted alphabetically.

To ensure that each weight is assigned to the correct ticker in the portfolio, we have to use a mapping dictionary, containing tickers and their corresponding weights. This dictionary is then converted into a Pandas Series and rearranged to match the order of tickers in the portfolio. This guarantees that the intended weights are applied correctly for each portfolio member.

# distribution of cash by weigths
weights = {
    'PFE': 0.4,
    'GOOG': 0.3,
    'MSFT': 0.2,
    'MRNA': 0.1
}
weights = pd.Series(weights)
weights = weights.reindex(close.columns)

total_cash = 10_000
init_cash_per_ticker = weights * total_cash

# generation of a baseline portfolio instance (multiple tickers)
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,                    # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries=long_entries,           # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits=long_exits,               # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    #short_entries=short_entries,   # no short-entry
    #short_exits=short_exits,       # no short-exit
    init_cash=init_cash_per_ticker, # init_cash is distributed by weights
    freq='D'                        # daily evaluation of metrics
)

By plotting the absolute value development of the portfolio members, we can confirm that the weights have been assigned correctly.

If you choose not to use the methodology outlined above, it is essential to verify by other means that the weights are properly allocated. I strongly recommend always performing a double-check to avoid unintended allocation errors.

In the plot below you can see, that our 10,000 EURO initial cash is correctly allocated, according to the weights.

Note: No Cash Pooling in our case:

When cash pooling is disabled, each ticker maintains its own isolated cash bucket. All purchases for a given ticker are funded exclusively from its bucket, and any proceeds from closing positions are returned directly to the same bucket. Cash is never shared between tickers, ensuring that each asset operates independently in terms of capital allocation and returns.

Get Tobi Lux’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

This approach makes the portfolio behavior more predictable and avoids unintended interactions between tickers due to shared cash.

Portfolio with Cash Pool

Next, we create a portfolio with the following features:

  • Multi-ticker portfolio
  • Long-only positions
  • Identical initial cash allocation for each ticker
  • Cash pooling (i.e., cash is shared across tickers)

This portfolio can be generated as follows:

# Total cash for the shared pool
total_cash = 10_000

# generation of a baseline portfolio instance (multiple tickers)
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,                    # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries=long_entries,           # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits=long_exits,               # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    #short_entries=short_entries,   # no short-entry
    #short_exits=short_exits,       # no short-exit
    init_cash=total_cash,           # init_cash is distributed
    cash_sharing=True,              # one cash-pool for all tickers
    freq='D'                        # daily evaluation of metrics
)

Note: With cash pooling enabled, all tickers draw from a shared cash pool. This means that buying assets for one ticker can use available cash from any other ticker, and proceeds from closed positions are returned to the same pool. Cash pooling allows for more flexible capital allocation and can improve efficiency, but it also introduces interactions between tickers that do not exist in isolated cash setups.

Compared to pf.value() for a portfolio with cash_sharing=False—which returns a DataFrame showing cash values for each individual ticker—we observe a different behavior when cash pooling is enabled. In this case, pf.value() produces only a single column, labeled "group".

The reason is straightforward: with cash pooling, no cash is tied to any single ticker. Instead, all cash is available to the entire portfolio, so metrics like pf.value() and pf.cash() now reflect aggregated values across all tickers.

We can also visualize multiple parameters together by customizing the built-in vectorbt plots. For example, we can plot total portfolio value and cash on the same chart. In such a plot, when the portfolio is fully invested (long), cash is zero, and when it is flat, the total value equals the cash balance.

combined = pd.concat([
    pf.value().rename("Total Value"),
    pf.cash().rename("Cash")
], axis=1)

combined.vbt.plot(width=900, height=200,title='Total value and cash of the portfolio').show()

In contrast to cash, other portfolio parameters are still tracked per ticker. For example, the number of assets is always available individually for each ticker in the portfolio.

There are two ways to access these per-ticker parameters:

  1. Using group_by=False: When calling a portfolio method (e.g., pf.assets()), setting group_by=False returns a DataFrame with separate columns for each ticker, instead of aggregated values.
  2. Accessing a specific ticker directly: You can also query an individual ticker by indexing the portfolio object, for example, pf.assets()[ticker]. This returns the time series for that particular ticker only.

Both approaches allow you to inspect ticker-level behavior even in a portfolio with cash pooling, while aggregated methods like pf.value() and pf.cash() reflect the combined portfolio-level view.

# option 1
pf.asset_value(group_by=False).vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=250, 
    title='Asset value of each portfolio member'
    ).show()

The code for option 2 is as follows:

# option 2
for ticker in pf.wrapper.columns:
    np.sign(pf.assets()[ticker]).vbt.plot(
        width=1000, height=100, 
        title=f'Position Over Time – {ticker}', 
        ).show()

In Figure 14, we can clearly observe one effect of cash pooling. Since our portfolio is set up to always use 100% of the available cash for trading, only one ticker is held at a time.

This behavior is also evident in the plot showing the timely evolution of positions for individual tickers in the portfolio. It provides an intuitive view of how cash pooling influences position allocation over time.

Portfolio with Cash Pool and Order Size Limitation

Next, we create a even more complex portfolio instance with the following features:

  • Multi-ticker portfolio
  • Long-only positions
  • Identical initial cash allocation for each ticker
  • Cash pooling (shared cash across tickers)
  • Order size limits:
  • size_type='Percent', size=0.3 → only 30% of the available cash is used per trade
  • min_size=5 → a minimum of 5 assets per ticker must be traded

This setup allows us to control both the fraction of cash deployed per trade and the minimum trade size, providing more realistic constraints on position sizing.

Interaction with cash pooling: Even though all tickers share a common cash pool, the order size limits prevent a single trade from consuming the entire available cash. This ensures that capital is distributed more evenly across multiple positions, avoiding the scenario where one ticker dominates the portfolio simply because cash is fully pooled.

Consequence: Unlike the previous example with unrestricted cash pooling — where only one ticker was held at a time — this configuration allows more than one ticker to be in the portfolio simultaneously, since no single trade can fully exhaust the cash pool.

# Total cash-pool
total_cash = 10_000

# generation of portfolio-instance with cash-pool and oder-size-limitation
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,                    # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries=long_entries,           # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits=long_exits,               # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    #short_entries=short_entries,   # no short-entry
    #short_exits=short_exits,       # no short-exit
    size_type='Percent',            # setting size type
    size=0.3,                       # use 30% of available cash
    min_size=5,                     # trade 5 assets min-
    init_cash=total_cash,           # value of the cash pool
    cash_sharing=True,              # cash-pool, all tickers share the same cash
    freq='D',                       # daily evaluation of metrics
)

The changed behaviour can also be seen, when plotting the cash-curve:

Portfolio with Groups of Tickers

Now we create a portfolio with the following features:

  • Multi-ticker portfolio
  • Long-only positions
  • SMA/RSI strategy
  • Grouping of tickers into two groups: Tech and Pharma
  • Cash equally distributed between the groups
  • Cash pooling within each group (i.e., cash is shared among tickers in the same group)

Important note: The sequence of tickers in the Portfolio object is different from the original tickers list. To avoid confusion, we map each ticker to its group using a dictionary, following the same methodology we introduced earlier when assigning weights.

ABSOLUT IMPORTANT: We then have to cluster the tickers according to their group:

  • The Tech group contains the first set of tickers in the dictionary
  • The Pharma group contains the second set

The sequence of tickers in the dictionary (i.e., the keys) now defines the leading order in the Portfolio object.

Consequence: To ensure all inputs are aligned correctly, we need to rearrange all data and parameters according to this sequence. In the code, this reordered sequence is referred to as new_order.

# Mapping tickers → group, allocating cash → group
mapping = {'GOOG': 'Tech', 'MSFT': 'Tech',
           'MRNA': 'Pharma', 'PFE': 'Pharma'}
groups = list(set(mapping.values()))

total_cash = 20_000
init_cash_per_group = total_cash/len(groups)

# reordering of relevant data to new_order (by groups)
new_order = list(mapping.keys())
close = close[new_order]
long_entries = long_entries[new_order]
long_exits = long_exits[new_order]

# CrossCheck
group_by = group_by = pd.Series(mapping)
print('Final group_by:     ', group_by.values.tolist())
print('sequence of columns:', close.columns.tolist())
print('groups:             ', groups)
Final group_by:      ['Tech', 'Tech', 'Pharma', 'Pharma']
sequence of columns: ['GOOG', 'MSFT', 'MRNA', 'PFE']
groups:              ['Tech', 'Pharma']

Now we generate the portfolio-object:

# generation of portfolio-instance with cash-pool and grouping
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,                    # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries=long_entries,           # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits=long_exits,               # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    # short_entries=short_entries,  # no short entries
    # short_exits=short_exits,      # no short exits
    init_cash=init_cash_per_group,  # cash per group
    cash_sharing=True,              # group-wise-cash-pool, all group members share the same cash
    group_by=group_by,              # assign tickers to the groups
    freq='D',                       # daily evaluation of metrics
)

In the case of a portfolio with groups, methods like pf.value() return aggregated values per group, rather than per individual ticker. Each group’s total value reflects the combined positions and cash of all tickers within that group, providing a clear overview of group-level performance.

In the code block below, we demonstrate how to add a new trace showing the total aggregated value of the portfolio, combining all groups. This allows us to visualize overall portfolio performance alongside the group-level metrics.

fig = pf.value().vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=250, 
    title='Value of portfolio (Total and per Group)')
fig.add_scatter(
    x=pf.value().index, y=pf.value().sum(axis=1),
    mode="lines", name="Total Value", line=dict(width=3))
fig.show()

Portfolio with Cash Pool per Group, Using 30% of Available Cash per Order

Next, we implement a portfolio with the following features:

  • Multi-ticker portfolio
  • Long-only positions
  • SMA/RSI strategy
  • Grouping of tickers into two groups: Tech and Pharma
  • Cash pooling within each group (tickers share cash within the group)
  • Different initial cash allocations per group
  • Each trade uses 30% of the available cash in the corresponding group

The portfolio instance can be generated as follows:

# Mapping tickers → group, allocating cash → group
mapping = {'GOOG': 'Tech', 'MSFT': 'Tech',
           'MRNA': 'Pharma', 'PFE': 'Pharma'}
mapping_cash = {'Tech': 6000, 'Pharma': 4000}  # non uniform initial cash
group_by = pd.Series(mapping)
init_cash_per_group = pd.Series(mapping_cash)

# Reorder close/entries/exits according to groups
new_order = list(mapping.keys())
close = close[new_order]
long_entries = long_entries[new_order]
long_exits = long_exits[new_order]

# Build portfolio with groups and different inital cash per group
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,                    # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries=long_entries,           # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits=long_exits,               # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    # short_entries=short_entries,  # no short entries
    # short_exits=short_exits,      # no short exits
    size_type='Percent',            # use percent of available cash
    size=0.3,                       # 30%
    init_cash=init_cash_per_group,  # different initial-cash per group
    group_by=group_by,              # assign tickers to groups
    cash_sharing=True,              # group-wise-cash-pool, all group members share the same cash
    freq='D',                       # daily evaluation of metrics
)

Note on interaction with group cash pooling: Even though tickers within a group share a common cash pool, the 30% order size limit prevents a single trade from consuming all available cash. This allows multiple positions to exist simultaneously within the group, distributing capital more evenly and reducing the risk that one ticker dominates the group’s allocation.

When analyzing multiple portfolio parameters, we observe the following:

  • Some parameters exist only as aggregated values at the group level, such as cash.
  • Other parameters are tracked only at the individual ticker level.
  • Some ticker-level parameters can be aggregated to the group level if needed, providing a summarized view of group performance.

Understanding the distinction between group-level and ticker-level parameters is important when interpreting portfolio metrics, especially in portfolios with cash pooling and grouped tickers.

# examples of parameters available on Group-Leel and Portfolio Level
fig = pf.value().vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=150, 
    title='Portfolio Value (Total and per Group)')
fig.add_scatter(
    x=pf.value().index, y=pf.value().sum(axis=1),
    mode='lines', name='Total Value', line=dict(width=3))
fig.show()

pf.net_exposure().vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=150, 
    title='Net Exposure (per Group)', 
    ).show()

# examples of parameters available on single ticker levels     
pf.assets().vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=150, 
    title='Assets (per ticker)', 
    ).show()
pf.asset_value(group_by=False).vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=150, 
    title='Asset Value (per ticker)',
    ).show()

# example hot to aggregate parameters form single tickert so group 
pf.asset_value().vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=150, 
    title='Asset Value (per Group)',
    ).show()

Portfolios for Long/Short Strategies

Now let’s combine everything into a portfolio that supports both long and short trades.

We implement a portfolio with the following features:

  • Multi-ticker portfolio
  • Long and short positions, Support for long and short entry signals, Support for long and short exit signals
  • SMA/RSI strategy
  • Grouping of tickers into multiple groups (e.g. Tech and Pharma)
  • Equal or user-defined initial cash allocation per group
  • Cash pooling within each group (cash sharing among tickers of the same group), No cash sharing between groups

We apply the methodology already discussed and extend it to include short positions. In addition to long_entries and long_exits, we now also use the short_entries and short_exits DataFrames.

As with the other inputs, these DataFrames must be rearranged to match the portfolio’s ticker order. In particular, their columns need to follow the new_order introduced earlier when grouping tickers. Ensuring consistent ordering across all inputs is essential for correct portfolio construction and signal interpretation.

The portfolio object is generated as follows:

# Mapping tickers → group, allocating cash → group
mapping = {'GOOG': 'Tech', 'MSFT': 'Tech',
           'MRNA': 'Pharma', 'PFE': 'Pharma'}
groups = list(set(mapping.values()))
group_by = pd.Series(mapping)

mapping_cash = {'Tech': 6000, 'Pharma': 4000}
init_cash_per_group = pd.Series(mapping_cash)

# Reorder close/entries/exits
new_order = list(mapping.keys())
close = close[new_order]
long_entries = long_entries[new_order]
long_exits = long_exits[new_order]
short_entries = short_entries[new_order]
short_exits = short_exits[new_order]

# generating Portfolio instance
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close=close,
    entries=long_entries,
    exits=long_exits,
    short_entries=short_entries,
    short_exits=short_exits,
    accumulate=True,
    init_cash=init_cash_per_group,
    group_by=group_by,
    cash_sharing=True,
    freq='D'
)

Let’s recall what we discussed for long-only portfolios regarding signal conflicts. In a long–short portfolio, the situation becomes even more complex, since signals from both the long and the short side can occur on the same bar.

In a long-only system, we need to define the behavior when a long_entry and a long_exit signal occur simultaneously. As shown earlier, this behavior is controlled by the upon_long_conflict parameter.

In a fully fledged long–short portfolio, many more conflict scenarios can arise. These are regulated by a set of dedicated parameters:

  • upon_long_conflict Controls behavior when long_entry and long_exit occur on the same bar.
  • upon_short_conflictRegulates behavior when short_entry and short_exit occur on the same bar.
  • upon_dir_conflictRegulates behavior when long_entry and short_entry occur on the same bar.
  • upon_opposite_entryRegulates behavior when an entry signal in the opposite direction occurs while already in a position.

Note on upon_opposite_entry:
Assume we are currently long and receive a short_entry signal without a preceding long_exit. In this situation, several options are available:

  • Ignore (default): Do nothing
  • Close: Close the current position
  • CloseReduce: Close or reduce the current position (if accumulation=True)
  • Reverse: Close the current position and open a position in the opposite direction
  • ReverseReduce: Close or reduce the current position and build up an opposite position (if accumulation=True)

Together, these parameters form a powerful and flexible framework for defining how a long–short portfolio reacts to complex and potentially conflicting signals.

All previously introduced methods for printing or plotting portfolio parameters remain applicable in this setting. In a long–short portfolio, we can observe several characteristics that are specific to short positions. One example is that asset values become negative whenever a short position is held.

pf.assets().vbt.plot(
    width=900, height=250, 
    title='number of assets', 
    ).show()

Another example is the cash curve. In this case, we can observe that the cash held in the group-level pools may exceed the initial cash allocation. This is a direct consequence of cash inflows generated by short selling, where proceeds from selling borrowed assets increase the available cash.

Last but not lease show our timely evolution of positions of individual tickers in portfolio also short positions:

Summary

Step by step, we’ve been turning up the complexity of our portfolio setups. We started with simple multi-ticker portfolios and gradually added cash pooling, grouping, and finally long–short trading. Combined with the parameters discussed along the way, this gives you a lot of flexibility to mix and match features and model even fairly sophisticated trading strategies.

If you want to go deeper, we strongly encourage you to check out the notebooks in the accompanying GitHub repository. They include many more examples and experiments than we could fit into this (already quite long) blog post.

Parts 1–5 were meant as a general introduction. In the final part (Part 6), we’ll move on to practical applications like sector rotation, parameter optimization, and more.

Stay tuned — there’s more to come 🚀
